panels.title: At-a-glance
panels.description: Live proxy load and health snapshot. Check here first when investigating issues — these stat panels give an instant read on current system state.

panels.title: Active Requests
panels.description:
Number of requests currently being processed by the proxy (started but not yet completed). This is a real-time gauge, not a rate — it shows 'right now, N requests are in flight.'

HOW TO READ: Sudden spikes indicate traffic bursts or upstream slowdowns (requests pile up if the backend is slow). If it climbs continuously without dropping, the proxy may be stuck or the upstream is saturated.

GOOD: Low and stable, tracking expected concurrency (0–10 typical).
BAD: Continuously rising or pegged at max concurrency — investigate upstream latency.

panels.title: RPS
panels.description:
Requests per second over the selected window, from litellm_proxy_total_requests_metric_total (all HTTP requests including health checks). Use this to gauge real-time traffic volume. Compare with RPM (×60) for sanity.

GOOD: Matches expected traffic pattern.
BAD: Unexpected drop to zero (proxy down) or sudden 10× spike (retry storms, runaway clients).

panels.title: RPM
panels.description:
Requests per minute over the selected window. Same metric as RPS scaled to per-minute. Use this for capacity planning — compare against your MaaS provider's RPM limit.

GOOD: Below 70% of your RPM limit.
BAD: Approaching or exceeding the limit — expect 429 rate-limit errors.

panels.title: Error %
panels.description:
Failed requests as a percentage of total requests, from litellm_deployment_failure_responses_total (deployment-level failures: 429 rate limits, 500 server errors, timeouts). This is the primary error health indicator.

GOOD: <1% (green) — healthy error rate.
WARNING: 1–5% (yellow) — elevated, monitor closely.
BAD: >5% (red) — investigate upstream provider status, rate limits, or model availability.

panels.title: TPS
panels.description:
Tokens per second (input + output combined) over the selected window, from litellm_total_tokens_metric_total. Measures throughput in terms of token generation. A model generating long outputs will have high TPS even with low RPS.

GOOD: Matches expected workload volume.
BAD: Sudden drops suggest request failures or model unavailability.

panels.title: TPM
panels.description:
Tokens per minute (input + output combined). Same as TPS scaled to per-minute. Use for provider TPM limit capacity planning.

GOOD: Below 70% of your provider's TPM limit.
BAD: Approaching the limit — expect throttling.

panels.title: Models Healthy
panels.description:
Count of distinct models with at least one deployment in healthy state (deployment_state == 0). A model is healthy when its upstream deployments respond successfully to health checks.

GOOD: Equals the total number of configured models.
BAD: Less than total — one or more models have degraded or unavailable deployments. Check the Deployment health table for details.

panels.title: Spend (window)
panels.description:
Total USD spent in the currently selected Grafana time range, from litellm_spend_metric_total. This is cumulative spend over the visible window, not a rate. Use it to quickly assess cost impact during incidents or traffic spikes.

GOOD: Within budget expectations.
BAD: Unexpectedly high — check for runaway clients, retry loops, or expensive model selection.

panels.title: Latency
panels.description: Streaming and request latency metrics per model. These panels reveal where time is spent in the request lifecycle: queue wait → proxy overhead → upstream API → first token → token generation. Use them to identify bottlenecks and compare model performance.

panels.title: TTFT by model
panels.description:
Time To First Token (P95) — the time from request start to the first token being streamed back, from litellm_llm_api_time_to_first_token_metric. This is what users perceive as 'the model is thinking.' It includes queue wait, proxy overhead, and the upstream's prefill phase.

GOOD: <1s for chat models, <2s for reasoning models — users perceive instant response.
BAD: >3s — users perceive lag; check Queue wait and LLM API latency panels to isolate the cause.

panels.title: TPOT by model
panels.description:
Time Per Output Token (P95) — the average time between consecutive output tokens during streaming, from litellm_deployment_latency_per_output_token. This measures the model's raw generation speed. Lower means faster token generation.

GOOD: <50ms/token for fast models, <100ms for reasoning models — smooth streaming.
BAD: >200ms — slow generation; may indicate provider throttling, model overload, or GPU contention.

panels.title: End-to-end latency
panels.description:
Total request latency P95 (seconds) — the full round-trip from request received to response completed, from litellm_request_total_latency_metric. P95 captures the slow tail that affects user experience.

GOOD: <2s for chat models, <5s for reasoning models.
BAD: >5s — check Queue wait, LLM API latency, and Proxy overhead to isolate the bottleneck.

panels.title: LLM API latency
panels.description:
Upstream LLM API call latency P95 (seconds) — time spent waiting for the model provider, from litellm_llm_api_latency_metric. This excludes proxy overhead and queue wait. Compare with End-to-end latency to isolate where time is spent.

GOOD: Close to End-to-end latency (proxy adds minimal overhead).
BAD: Much higher than expected — provider is slow, rate-limiting, or experiencing an outage.

panels.title: Proxy overhead
panels.description:
LiteLLM proxy processing overhead (P95) — time spent inside the proxy itself (routing, auth, logging, callbacks), from litellm_overhead_latency_metric. This is the difference between end-to-end latency and upstream API latency.

GOOD: <100ms — proxy is not a bottleneck.
BAD: >500ms — proxy is slow; check callback handlers, logging volume, or CPU saturation on the proxy container.

panels.title: Queue wait
panels.description:
Request queue wait time (P95) — how long requests wait in the LiteLLM queue before being dispatched to the upstream, from litellm_request_queue_time_seconds. This is zero under low load; it rises when all deployment slots are busy.

GOOD: <100ms — no queuing pressure.
BAD: >1s — the proxy is saturated; consider adding more deployments, increasing concurrency limits, or adding API keys.

panels.title: Errors & Health
panels.description: Error rates, status code breakdown, deployment state, and per-model health. Use these panels to diagnose failures and identify degraded deployments.

panels.title: Errors by model
panels.description:
Absolute count of failed requests per model in the selected time window, from litellm_deployment_failure_responses_total using increase() over $__range. Each line shows the total failure count for one model.

HOW TO READ: This is a cumulative count over the visible time range, not a rate. Spikes indicate model-specific issues (rate limits, model unavailable, provider outage).

GOOD: All lines at or near zero.
BAD: Sustained non-zero count for a model — check provider status, rate limits, and model configuration.

panels.title: Error status codes
panels.description:
Distribution of HTTP status codes for failed requests in the selected time window, from litellm_deployment_failure_responses_total grouped by exception_status.

STATUS CODE REFERENCE:
  400 — Bad Request: malformed request, invalid model name, or bad parameters.
  401 — Unauthorized: API key invalid, expired, or missing.
  403 — Forbidden: API key lacks permission for the requested model or action.
  404 — Not Found: requested model or endpoint does not exist.
  408 — Timeout: request exceeded the configured timeout limit.
  429 — Rate Limited: too many requests — hit MaaS provider RPM/TPM limit. Transient; should recover automatically via cooldown/retry.
  500 — Internal Server Error: upstream provider returned an unexpected error. Investigate provider status.
  502 — Bad Gateway: upstream provider unreachable or returned invalid response. Provider may be down.
  503 — Service Unavailable: upstream temporarily overloaded. Usually transient.
  504 — Gateway Timeout: upstream did not respond in time. Provider is slow or unresponsive.

GOOD: Empty (no errors) or dominated by 429 (transient rate limits that auto-recover).
BAD: Large 500/502/503/504 slices — upstream provider issues requiring investigation.

targets.expr: label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(label_replace(sum by (exception_status) (increase(litellm_deployment_failure_responses_total{requested_model=~"$model"}[$__range])), "status", "$1", "exception_status", "(.*)"), "status", "400 — Bad Request", "exception_status", "400"), "status", "401 — Unauthorized", "exception_status", "401"), "status", "403 — Forbidden", "exception_status", "403"), "status", "404 — Not Found", "exception_status", "404"), "status", "408 — Timeout", "exception_status", "408"), "status", "429 — Rate Limited", "exception_status", "429"), "status", "500 — Internal Server Error", "exception_status", "500"), "status", "502 — Bad Gateway", "exception_status", "502"), "status", "503 — Service Unavailable", "exception_status", "503"), "status", "504 — Gateway Timeout", "exception_status", "504")

panels.title: Deployment state
panels.description:
Deployment health state per model over time, from litellm_deployment_state. Values: 0=healthy, 1=partial degradation (some deployments down), 2=outage (all deployments down). Each line represents a model's current health state.

GOOD: All lines at 0 — all models fully healthy.
BAD: Any line at 1 (partial — some deployments degraded) or 2 (outage — model is down).

panels.title: Throughput & Capacity
panels.description: Request and token throughput rates, traffic distribution across models, and RPM utilization against provider limits. Use these to understand load patterns and capacity headroom.

panels.title: Total Requests
panels.description:
Absolute count of all requests (successful + failed) across all deployments. This is a cumulative counter, not a rate — use it to understand total volume over the proxy's lifetime.

GOOD: Increases steadily, with failures being a small fraction.
BAD: Stagnant (no traffic) or failures dominate the count.

panels.title: Successful Requests
panels.description:
Absolute count of successful responses across all deployments. Compare with Total Requests and Failed Requests to compute success rate.

GOOD: Close to Total Requests (>99%).
BAD: Significantly less than Total — check error panels for the failure cause.

panels.title: Failed Requests
panels.description:
Absolute count of failed responses across all deployments. This is the raw failure count, useful for understanding error volume (not just percentage).

GOOD: 0 or very low.
BAD: High and growing — sustained failures; check Error rate by model for per-model details.

panels.title: RPM by model
panels.description:
Requests per minute broken down by model, from litellm_proxy_total_requests_metric_total. Each line shows traffic volume for one model. Use this to understand which models receive the most traffic and spot usage pattern changes.

GOOD: Matches expected traffic distribution.
BAD: One model suddenly dominates (misconfigured client) or a model drops to zero (clients can't reach it).

panels.title: TPM by model
panels.description:
Tokens per minute broken down by model, from litellm_total_tokens_metric. Shows token consumption per model. Compare with RPM by model — a model with low RPM but high TPM has long conversations.

GOOD: Matches expected usage pattern.
BAD: Unexpected spikes — runaway clients or unexpectedly long outputs.

panels.title: Tokens
panels.description: Token usage broken down by type: input (prompt), output (completion), and reasoning (thinking). Use these to understand cost drivers and model behavior.

panels.title: Input tokens
panels.description:
Input (prompt) tokens per minute per model, from litellm_input_tokens_metric. Input tokens are what the client sends — prompts, context, system instructions. They drive prefill latency and cost.

GOOD: Proportional to request rate.
BAD: Disproportionately high — clients sending excessive context (large prompts, untrimmed conversation history).

panels.title: Output tokens
panels.description:
Output (completion) tokens per minute per model, from litellm_output_tokens_metric. Output tokens are the model's response. They drive generation time and cost.

GOOD: Proportional to expected response length.
BAD: Unexpectedly high — model generating excessively long responses; check max_tokens settings.

panels.title: Reasoning tokens
panels.description:
Reasoning (thinking) tokens per minute per model, from litellm_output_reasoning_tokens_metric. These are internal tokens generated by reasoning models (chain-of-thought) before the final response. They consume time and cost but are not visible to the user.

GOOD: Expected for reasoning models; near-zero for non-reasoning models.
BAD: Unexpectedly high for non-reasoning models (misconfigured) or dominating total output (expensive reasoning overhead).

panels.title: Cost
panels.description: Spend accumulation and rate per model. Use these to track cost trends and spot runaway spending in real time.

panels.title: Total cost
panels.description:
Cumulative USD spent across all models since the proxy started, from litellm_spend_metric_total. This is a lifetime counter — use it for budget tracking.

GOOD: Within budget.
BAD: Approaching or exceeding budget — check Spend rate panel for real-time cost trajectory.

panels.title: Cost per model
panels.description:
Cumulative USD spend over time per model. Each line shows the running total for one model. Use this to identify which models cost the most over time.

GOOD: Linear growth at expected rate.
BAD: Sudden steep slope — runaway costs on a model; check for expensive model selection or excessive usage.

panels.title: Spend rate
panels.description:
USD per minute per model — the derivative of cumulative spend, from litellm_spend_metric_total. This is the real-time cost rate. Use it to spot runaway costs immediately rather than waiting for cumulative totals.

GOOD: Stable and within expected rate.
BAD: Sudden spikes — a client switched to an expensive model or is making excessive high-cost requests.
